title: Cat Fluid Filled Blister on Cornea: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your cat's eye bulging? Learn about corneal blisters (bullous keratopathy), urgency levels, and why a clear photo helps your vet triage the issue.
content_html:
TL;DR: A clear bubble on a cat's eye is a serious condition requiring veterinary care within 12 to 24 hours to prevent potential rupture and permanent vision loss.

What does it mean if my cat has a clear bubble on their eye?

Seeing a clear, fluid-filled bubble on your cat's eye can be very alarming. This is often a condition known as bullous keratopathy, where fluid accumulates between the layers of the cornea. It can also represent a descemetocele, which is a very deep corneal ulcer that has progressed to the eye's innermost membrane. Both conditions signify that the structural integrity of the eye is compromised.

How quickly should I take my cat to the vet for an eye blister?

  • Seek veterinary care within 12 to 24 hours, as this is a localized emergency for the eye.
  • Understand that these blisters are extremely fragile; if they rupture, it can lead to the collapse of the eyeball and permanent vision loss.
  • Be aware that your cat is likely experiencing significant discomfort, even if they are masking it well.

How does sending a photo help my veterinarian triage my cat's eye condition?

  • Allows the triage team to see the exact size and location of the blister and determine how active the lesion looks.
  • Helps the clinic prioritize your cat's case based on the visual severity.
  • Enables staff to prepare necessary diagnostic tools—like fluorescein stain or a tonometer—before you even walk through the door.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, corneal lacerations are infrequent but can result from bites or self-inflicted trauma, potentially leading to partial or full-thickness corneal defects. Clinical signs of full-thickness lacerations include pain, blepharospasm, tearing, corneal defects, and possible iris prolapse, often accompanied by aqueous flare, hyphema, miosis, and pupillary distortion. Underlying systemic diseases and pathogens such as feline herpesvirus-1 should be considered, as they can manifest as ulcerative or stromal keratitis. Full-thickness corneal ulcers, sometimes with iris prolapse, are addressed with conjunctival grafts, followed by topical and systemic broad-spectrum antibiotics, NSAIDs or corticosteroids, and mydriatics, tapered over 4-8 weeks to manage potential complications like corneal scarring, synechiae, cataract formation, or bacterial endophthalmitis.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1698)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat's eye blister?
faqs.answer: No, you should never apply human medications to a cat's eye. Some human drops contain ingredients that can worsen a corneal injury or cause severe allergic reactions in cats.

faqs.question: Will a corneal blister heal on its own?
faqs.answer: It is highly unlikely. Without professional treatment, these blisters usually progress or rupture. Medical intervention is necessary to manage pain and prevent infection.

faqs.question: Should I try to clean the eye at home?
faqs.answer: Do not touch or rub the blister, as the cornea is very thin in that area and can easily pop. If there is discharge, you may very gently wipe the fur around the eye with a damp cloth, but avoid the eyeball itself.
